votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the sum and product calculation using a loop, which produces the same result as the Coq fold_right operations due to the associativity and commutativity of integer addition and multiplication.

The Python code computes the sum and product of the list elements, which matches the Coq fold_right operations since addition and multiplication are commutative and associative, and correctly checks equality with the given result pair.
The Python code correctly computes the sum and product of the list elements and compares them to the given result tuple, matching the Coq specification.

Both compute sum using addition with identity 0 and product using multiplication with identity 1 over the list elements. The Coq fold_right and Python iterative loop produce the same results for addition and multiplication since these operations are associative. The final comparison res == (s, p) matches the Coq equality check.

Both compute sum using addition with identity 0 and product using multiplication with identity 1 over the list elements. The Coq fold_right and Python iterative loop produce the same results for addition and multiplication since these operations are associative. The final comparison res == (s, p) matches the Coq equality check.

Both compute sum using addition with identity 0 and product using multiplication with identity 1 over the list elements. Coq uses fold_right while Python uses a for loop with accumulation, but both produce the same sum and product for any concrete list of integers, then check if res equals the tuple (sum, product).
Both compute the sum (0 identity) and product (1 identity) over the list and check equality with res; Python’s left-to-right loop matches Coq’s right fold for associative operations on integers.
Both compute the sum (init 0) and product (init 1) of the list and check res equals (s, p). Python ints are unbounded like Coq Z, so results coincide for all inputs.

Both compute the sum and product of the list with neutral elements 0 and 1 and check equality with res; associativity of Z.add/mul makes Python’s iteration match Coq’s fold_right, including on empty lists.
